BUT HOME FRONT IS UNHAPPY been invited, but not Egypt. Subsequently, Ghana had joined Israel in a joint shipping venture (Black Star shipping line), and begun other economic cooperation. But with Egypt's President Gamal Abdel Nasser in firm control of the Suez Canal, there was need for quick action to keep Black Star ships moving on the seven seas. Taking an Egyptian bride brought Prime Minister Nasser's blessings. But though the alliance may have bolstered Nkrumah's hand abroad, things were less happy at home. He was being pressed at every turn by Professor Kofi A. Busia, leader of the opposition United Party, who in Chicago last week disclosed that all was not well with the new nation. Busia's chief beef: Ghana has no bill of rights, guaranteeing protection of the individual; several deportations have been ordered, from which the exiles had no possible appeal (the trio included one newsman) ; the new political system ignores the deep roots of the former system of government by tribal chiefs (who, unlike Nkrumah, could be instantly voted out of office) . 15

I i JET EXCLUSIVE McGolUun TALKS; By MARC CRAWFORD Seated along the wall of the high-ceilinged conference   room of the Florida State Hospital for the criminally insane were three women.   Two were uniformed nurses, one white, the other Negro. The third woman, dressed in a colored frock of pencil-lined stripes, looked as if she were en route to a PTA tea. She was Ruby McCollum. "I'm glad to see you," she said, rising. Her voice was i whiny, like that of a 10-year-old girl. She shook hands I warmly, then smoothed her dress as she reseated herself. I Her eyes sparkled as though she was slightly amused about some secret. And as she smiled, the light danced j on her gold-crowned teeth and rhinestone earrings. I Her greeting to a JET reporter was the first word she had spoken to a newspaperman since 1952 when she shot I to death Clifford LeRoy Adams Jr., Live Oak, Florida's most prominent white man. Ruby stroked her freshly scrubbed face, apologized for not having put on powder and lipstick. "I have no one to make-up for anymore," she explained. Then she fell silent for a moment, studied the burnt yellow moccasins she 48 JETs interview with Ruby McCollum marked the first time she talked to the outside world since she killed Dr. Clifford Adams on Aug. 3, 1952.

REPORTER CRACKS CENSORSHIP was wearing as though reflecting on the truth of her last statement. Indeed, Ruby did not have anyone to make-up for. Both of her men were dead. Six years before on Aug. 3, 1952, mahogany-brown, 3 7 -yearold Ruby McCollum could make no other Ruby killed Dr. Adams in his colored waiting room. Ruby, sentenced to death in 1952, leaves court in Live Oak, claim to fame, perhaps, except that she was the wife of the wealthiest Negro in obscure Live Oak, Fla., and pregnant a second time by the county's most prominent white man— Clifford LeRoy Adams Jr., state senator and physician. But that day, she became a nationally famous woman. She shot Dr. Adams to death at 11:34 that Sunday morning. 49 Cl] dterial

Arrow points to where Ruby lives in dormitory with 150 other Negro women. They are friendly, she says, hut have no privacy. Ruby's Husband Made $300,000 In Numbers Racket Her husband, "Bolita" Sam, king of the Suwannee County numbers racket and a shrewd businessman who had parlayed nothing into more than $300,000, died the next day. From a "heart attack," the record says. Actually, Ruby confided in her Negro attorney, Releford McGriff, she had lost Sam back in 1948 when she bore Dr. Adams a daughter. She lost him (though they continued to live together) the morning Dr. Adams delivered Loretta, called Sam into the bedroom and told him: "Sam, I love all my children — niggers and white — and this is my baby. I want you to treat it right and see that it gets proper care. And I don't want you bothering Ruby, do you understand?" Sam understood. He understood so well that he would get up and leave whenever the doctor came to his house — so well that he didn't even go home when he spied the doctor's car in the McCollum driveway. And pot-bellied, 270-pound Dr. Adams? While the town's 50

whites and Negroes packed their churches praying for the promise of Heaven, Ruby was in the doctor's office, sending him straight to his reward with five well-aimed bullets from a .38-caliber pistol. She was sentenced to die in the electric chair, then granted a new trial, but was finally adjudged criminally insane after spending two years in the Suwannee County Jail. Last week, at the Florida State Hospital in Chattahoochee, JET became the first publication to interview Ruby McCollum since she fired those angry shots nearly six years ago. It was only through the intercession of Florida's Gov. LeRoy Collins that this was accomplished after six days of conferences with the McCollum family and Florida officials. Seated there in the conference room, the fingers of her left hand drumming nervous rhythm on her upper right arm, Ruby said: "If I could go home and get plenty rest, satisfaction, vitamins and food I like — I would look better than I do now." Yet, she looked better than at any time since her ordeal began. Her black hair was freshly pressed, parted in the center and drawn back to the base of her skull where it met three horizontal rows of tightly wound curls. Her complexion was radiant and healthy. "The food here is good," she said, "and they treat me all right, but I'd like to be home with my family. I don't get a chance to hear the radio or see television, ^.^^ ^j^^^^ entrance to building so I crochet a lot — where Ruby has lived for three years. 51 Cui , aterial

1 Would Like To Go Back To Live Oak,' Says Ruby doilies and things. "They have movies here on Saturday nights and I read papers and magazines. Yes, I know what the sputnik is. It's a Russian scientific invention that travels in outer space." Ruby's eyes fell again on her yellow shoes for a moment and she laughed. "You know I've gained 30 pounds since I've been here," she volunteered. "I weigh 159 now. Will you tell all the people who prayed for me I said 'thank you, so much?' " Ruby said she prays, but has only attended Sunday services in the basement four times in her three years there. Now she was talking about the two years spent at Fessenden Academy. "You know it isn't there anymore. They sold it." There was pride in her voice as she added: "I studied nursing there for two years. I've always wanted to relieve suffering." Then she talked about her children. Her eyes grew wistful, as if there was a terrible crying inside without tears. "The oldest and youngest girls are living with my sister in New Jersey. They write to. me often. I want to see them and be with them so bad, I just don't know what to do. The middle girl lives in Osceola, Fla., with my brother. And Sam Jr. still lives in Live Oak. When I get out of here I'm going back to Live Oak to live. Isn't it a nice place? I've been to Chicago, Detroit and I've lived in Poughkeepsie, N. Y., but I love Live Oak better than any place I've ever been." Ruby also talked about her son, Sam Jr., who inherited some of his father's interests and has about 12 juke boxes, from which he collects money. "Sam Jr. comes to see me sometimes, and he sent me a box of chocolate candy for Christmas. Wasn't that nice of him?" She couldn't remember, however, the last time Sam Jr. visited her. Neither could anyone in Live Oak. And while Sam Jr. had no objections to accepting what he could from the fast-dwindling family fortune, he said, "I hope I never hear the name McCollum again. You don't 52

know what it's like to be pointed out everywhere you go. ' You don't know what it's like to pick up a newspaper or l magazine and find the whole mess staring you in the face. It's never happened to you or to your family, and I have to carry this name to the grave. My mother's up there and leave her be." And from across the street, where Ruby's brother ' Matthew Jackson lives, came more of the same sentiment. "I love my sister, but I don't want to see her. I'm trying to pick up the pieces of my life now and go on living. A ' man can only stand so much without cracking up." There | was pleading in the gentle eyes of the big farmer. Matthew had carried Ruby's meals to the Suwannee County Jail three times a day for two years because Ruby did not want the prison fare. And during the last year, as Ruby went "stir crazy," she cursed her jailor daily and called him "poor white cracker trash." She also cursed Matthew, tormented him, accused him of trying to poison her. Matthew had to sample all food and drink in her presence. But he never stopped coming. **She was my sister, what could I do?" Now Ruby was relaxed, no longer hugged herself Indian fashion. She recalled the Joe Louis-Bob P a s tor championship fight she attended in 1939 at Detroit. 53 C^i , - ateriaL

Ruby Remembers touis-Pastor Fight In Detroit "I called Pastor 'Bicycle Bob' because Louis had to chase him for 11 rounds before he could catch up with him. Detroit's a nice place, but not like Live Oak." The nurses began shifting their feet, and the superintendent, Dr. W. B. Rogers, grew restless. Finally, Ruby rose, shook hands, then gave each of her nurses one of her hands. She stared for a minute, and the nurses moved her to the door. Over her shoulder. Ruby McCoUum called back in a wee voice: "Goodbye." German Music Students Boo Soprano Vera Little Mezzo soprano Vera Little was booed at her opera debut by West German music students in a demonstration aimed at the director of West Berlin's Municipal Opera, Carl Ebert. Miss Little, appearing in the title role of Carmen, was heckled with shouts of "Little, go home!" and "Ami (American) go home!" by students in the top balcony. The singer, who went to Europe four years ago after appearing Miss Little with the New York Philharmonic and the Philadelphia Academy of Music, declared after the show: "I am not upset. SHIRLEY BASSEY ADMITS GIVING Amidst court-aired charges that she jilted a 20-year-old British society scion and "got rid of four babies by different men," 20-year-old London singing star Shirley Bassey made a tearful admission. Said she: "Yes, I did have a child three years ago and I vowed then that I would never reveal my secret. But now I must tell the facts." The facts, as Miss Bassey told them: "I had one unfortunate romance in my life and I have been paying the penalty ever since. It happened when I was 17 and touring with a show called Memories Of Jolson. I met a boy and we thought we were in love. A few From tawny Shirley Bas- months later I realized I was sey, a tearful admission. going to have a baby. The boy wanted me to get rid of it and when I refused, he vanished and I have never seen or heard from him since." The singer said she stayed with the show as long as she could, putting a little money aside for her unborn child. "Then I had to give up. I couldn't carry on any longer and I went away to have my baby. She is a lovely little girl I named Sharon." With the birth of her child, Miss Bassey declared, she saw her dreams of a career and stardom smashed, until her present manager, Michael Sullivan, persuaded her to return to the stage. For a while, she still refused, working as a waitress until she decided she wasn't making enough money. 60

BIRTH TO DAUGHTER, NOW 3 "I gave little Sharon to my married sister to look after," she said, "and I went back to work. It broke my heart to leave her . . . Sharon is three years old now and she calls me Auntie Shirley. She must never know the real truth — that I am her mother." Miss Bassey said she has bought a house in her daughter's name and has set up a trust fund for her. "People have been saying dreadful things about me," the singer sobbed. "They have said I got rid of four babies. They are suggesting that I was wild and irresponsible and went with all sorts of fellows. It's all untrue." The charges were made by the mother of Terence C. Davis, a young Briton who, embittered because he lost Shirley's love, held her at gunpoint for several hours in a London hotel last November and fired at police. Davis said he acted in a fit of jealousy, and was prodded by his mother's attacks on Shirley's character. He got a three-year jail term. Miss Bassey made the confession about her daughter in Sydney, Australia, after collapsing in the wings of the Tivoli Theater upon hearing reports of Davis' trial. Said she later: "Why do they call me a bad girl? Why do they want to make me suffer for one little mistake I made when I was so Davis, Sharon and Shirley in young? happier days.

BACK FROM RUSSIA, JET PUBLISHER Any Negro who feels that communism is the solution to the race problem in the United States will be sadly disappointed if he takes a trip to Soviet Russia. This is the opinion of John H. Johnson, publisher of JET and EBONY Magazines, who with his wife, Eunice, was a member of the press group which accompanied Vice President Nixon on his recent trip to Russia and Poland. There Dixie Whites Value Money Above Segregation "White Southerners are not in effect for segregation when it means losing something they value even more than the separation of the races," writes Spelman College history department head, Dr. Howard Zinn, in Harper's magazine. Dr. Zinn claims most southerners rank money, law and order and the maintenance of their reputation for good manners ahead of segregation preference. White Boy, 12, Drowns Trying To Save Ark. Negro, 14 A 12-year-old white boy drowned in Parkin, Ark., while trying to save a floundering Negro youth who was later rescued from a plantation swimming hole. Walter Edward Holmes died after leaping to the aid of Joe Duckett, 14, who went under while trying to swim across a waterfilled ditch with the aid of two other Negroes. Another Negro youth, Odell Worthem, 12, tried to save both boys but failed as Duckett was brought to safety. 4 Mr. Nixon chats with Mr. & Mrs. John H. Johnson at U. S. Embassy in Moscow.

I SAYS COMMUNISM NOT FOR NEGROES are relatively few Negroes in the Soviet Union, and Mr. Johnson agrees with the Russian claim that it does not have a race problem. However, he is quick to point out that most of the privileges which we take for granted, such as freedom of speech and press and the right to own property, are not enjoyed by the Russian people. "The American Negro who wants better housing, more productive employment, and more individual opportunities will find that they are not available to him in Russia because they are not available to the majority of the Russian people." Mr. Johnson does feel, however, that even though the communist philosophy is not a good one for Negro or white Americans, there is no reason why the United States and Russia should not be friendly and peaceful. He believes that the Nixon trip contributed much to the development of friendly relations between the two countries and that further cultural and educational exchanges should be continued. Dred Scott's Great Grandson Named Mich. Police Sgt. Dred Scott Madison, great grandson of the pre-Civil War slave who sued for his freedom, was promoted to sergeant in the Grand Rapids, Mich., police force and became the second Negro to become a superior officer. A policeman since 1947, Madison, 38, participated in a 1958 St. Louis re-enactment of the Dred Scott trial in which the court ruled that a slave did not gain freedom by living in a free state. Hold Tallahassee Deputy On Larceny Charge Tripped by $200 in marked bills he accepted from a youth on the pretense of clearing him of an already dismissed burglary charge, a Tallahassee, Fla., deputy constable, James Williams, was arrested when the money was found on his person. Freddie Patten, 19, accused in a burglary case, helped police trip up the 48-year-old constable, who admitted accepting the money. 5 Cop

WHAT NEGROES DON'T KNOW ABOUT HAWAII 2,500 NEGROES FIND BIAS About two years ago, pretty, 23-yearold Barbara Crutchfield, a law graduate from the University of Iowa, applied for and got a job teaching law at the University of Hawaii, on the Island of Oahu. As she prepared to leave the U. S. for her Hawaiian assignment, the image of swaying palm trees, balmy trade winds, and natives basking Lawyer says Hawaiians don't want outside grass huts to be referred to as "foreigners." while hula girls constantly entertained them caused her no end of concern. She fretted about carrying enough clothes and supplies that would be needed in what she thought would be her new "primitive environment." When she arrived, however, her concern abruptly dissolved into wide-eyed astonishment. There were no grass shacks. There were no hula girls. No natives lying around. Instead, she found a modern city, not at all different from any other in the U. S. Today, after two years of living in Hawaii, and giving up her teaching post to take a job in Los Angeles in the legal department of a real estate firm, she smiles at her own former misconceptions of the 50th state, yet feels there are millions who still share similar ignorance. To begin with, she points out, many in the U. S. commit the most common error of referring to the people of Hawaii as "foreigners." This they resent. "There are Orientals and a large number of people of Polynesian descent," Barbara 6

'NON-EXISTENT' IN 50th STATE explains, "but this does not mean they are foreign. Their allegiance is to the U. S., and they not only think of themselves as Americans, but are, in fact, American. They refer to the continental U. S. as 'the mainland/ and resent it being called 'the States* or 'America/ " Another common false notion is that the people of Hawaii speak a different language. Not true, says Barbara. "There is a Hawaiian language," she explains, "but it is only spoken by older Hawaiians. Everyone speaks English, with the exception of some of the very old Orientals. The younger generation speaks English almost to the exclusion of any other language." As for the hula girls and grass skirts, they're just not around unless you're traveling with the tourists to night clubs or happen to be fortunate enough to be invited to a luau (a feast). Hula dancing is strictly a night club attraction and grass skirts are unheard of for street wear. The average tourist, however, feels that Hawaii would not be Copyrighted material

Hawaiians Proud Of Racial Mixtures Hawaii if he did not find this and occasionally may proceed to perform his own version. Barbara recalls once being in the company of some white tourists who had learned to hula while coming over on the boat, and finding no such dancing in the night club they visited, proceeded to put on a stage show. They did — in the presence of some 250 Hawaiians. When they finished the applause was loud. Then they did an encore. But out of their earshot in the audience were knowing smiles and back-ofhand whispers: "stupid haoles (the Hawaii word for white). " For a state that has its share of drive-in movies, hot dog stands, department stores and five-and-dimes, Hawaii is different, perhaps, only in that it probably has more mixed marriages than anywhere else in the U. S. "I found the race problem almost non-existent,,, observed Barbara. "There are so many races and racial mixtures that people view it with no surprise." As an example of race mixing, she says it is not uncommon to find children who admit proudly to a mixture of sometimes as many as 10 racial strains. And if you ask them what they are, they are likely to rattle off a hyphenated genealogy of all 10. Proof of the Hawaiian's pride of race, however, can best be shown in the annual Ka Palapala Beauty Pageant at the University of Hawaii where six beauty queens are a Copyrighted material

chosen simultaneously. "It is felt," explains Barbara, "that each race has its own distinct type of beauty and therefore should not compete one against the other." Hence, six "queens," and among them one category called "cosmopolitan," which is a classification for those mixed racially. As for the territory's estimated 2,500 Negro residents, they find segregation non-existent. There is no segregation in any of the business establishments. Neither do Negroes live in any particular area. They find homes in any neighborhood, often with a Japanese family as one next-door neighbor or a Hawaiian-Portuguese family for the other. Any mass congregation of Negroes in any one area is usually done by Negro servicemen who may come in town on leave from the island's many bases. Indeed Hawaii is a virtual tropical paradise, but it also has its less appealing facets. Among them: high living costs and low wages. For example: rent ($85-$90 for a IV2 to two-room apt.); gasoline (43 cents per gallon). But it does have its blue skies and year-round 70-degree weather. The only complaint: many feel far removed from the mainland and tend to feel confined in the tiny area. Consequently, those who leave often do so because of this feeling of claustrophobia. Dont's For Americans In Hawaii • Don't refer to Hawaiians as "foreigners." • Don't refer to the U. S. as "The States" or "America." Say: "The mainland." • Don't expect to find hula girls dancing everywhere. • Don't expect to find "natives" in grass skirts. • Don't look for grass huts, either. There aren't any. • Don't try to speak a "Hawaiian language." Speak English. Everybody else does. • Don't stare at mixed couples. Almost everybody's mixed. • Don't try to lead a hula line. 9 Copyrig

Del, Negro Family's 'Bombed Home' Demolished A $13,500 Negro family home in the all-white community of Collins Park, Del., near Wilmington, which was destroyed by a high velocity explosion on August 2, was demolished last week as being dangerous to public safety. DuPont Chemical plant laborer, George Rayfield, his wife Lucille and their 15year old daughter Geraldine are homeless as a result of the blast and now live with a Wilmington brother-in law, Charles Goode. The explosion, bclie\ed to be the work of professionals, drove the Rayfields out of the two-family brick residence they had been defying mob action to live in since February. In April, two weeks after they moved into the home, the Rayfield kitchen was bombed causing $3,500 in damage. Meanwhile, in week end arrests, state police and the FBI seized seven white men and obtained signed confessions from five of them that they were in the Rayfield home the night of the April blast. They were released in bail that totalled $55,000. THE CANDIDATES NEGROES PREFER FOR PRESIDENT NIXON, STEVENSON, HUMPHREY By SIMEON BOOKER With the important 1960 Presidential campaign coming up and the Negro vote a balance of power in some 10 key states, the most popular candidate for the nation's highest office today among U. S. Negroes is Vice President Richard Nixon. Scarcely known among minority groups when he first ran on the GOP ticket with President Eisenhower, the Californian has zoomed into an unmatched popularity among both Negro Democrats and Republicans. On the Democratic side, ex-Illinois Gov. Adlai Stevenson, a twotime contender for the Presidency, and Minneapolis Sen. Hubert H. Humphrey, long a civil rights fighter, rally the heaviest, but still not predominant, support. Despite what some politicians say, civil rights far exceeds economics as the major issue for campaigning among leading Negroes in 22 states. These views are made known in JET's first nationwide Nixon (I. with Nkrumah) is Republican big gun, Gov. Rockefeller (r. with Roy Wilkins) ran a distant second in exclusive JET poll. 14

SCORE HEAVILY IN NATIONAL POLL For Dems, Stevenson (I. with Gov. G. Mennen Williams) is sentimental favorite, but polled a tie with Sen. Hubert Humphrey. poll of Negro leaders. Some 855 leaders distributed in 22 states were queried as to their Presidential preference and the issues foremost to Negroes in the 1960 race. The survey included 200 leaders from each party who are recognized by their respective national committees, as well as a sampling of 455 outstanding spokesmen in education, business, religion and civic affairs from coast to coast. Enthusiasm among Negro Republicans far exceeded the Democratic response, a factor which indicates the degree of GOP positiveness and the high degree of indecision among the Negro Democrats on their party's standard bearer. The Republican leaders were asked: "Who do you prefer for president?" Among those who responded, the results were as follows: Richard Nixon 81.7% Nelson Rockefeller 18.3% The Negro Republicans were also asked: "Who do you 15

Says Nixon Has Clearer Insight Into Minority Problems prefer as the opposition candidate?" The response was as follows: Adlai Stevenson 26.7% John F. Kennedy 21.3% Hubert H. Humphrey 17.3% Lyndon Johnson 14.7% Stuart Symington 5.3% G. Mennen Williams 4.0% The more than 4-1 preference for the Vice President surprises the politicians who argue that the New York Gov. Nelson Rockefeller is a heavy favorite among Negroes because of his family's wide philanthropic endeavors. Nixon's support as the preferred candidate came from varied sources. A Fort Worth leader wrote: "Because of his just and impartial thinking." A Richmond, Va., pollster said: "He's experienced, both aggressive and progressive. A liberal." From Seattle, Wash.: "He's qualified and forthright." A Chicago leader said: "He'll do more for the Negro, which is uppermost in my mind at present." From Birmingham: "I think he's the best man the Republicans have." From Oklahoma City: "I believe his contacts with Negroes give him a clearer insight into minority problems." From Detroit: "He sets well in Asia and Africa." From Tuskegee: "He has the courage to act on vital questions and issues." Meanwhile, Gov. Rockefeller received endorsement from Symington Kennedy Brown Johnson 16 Copy rig I 

East St. Louis: "I like his liberal attitude toward members of our race." From Chicago: "The Negro businessman would get a better break." Although the Negro Republicans favored Nixon and endorsed Stevenson as an opposition candidate, there was no such clear cut selection of a Democratic candidate among Negro Democrats. Out of 200 Negro Democrats who were asked the same question as to their Presidential preferences, the following results were obtained: Adlai Stevenson 33.8% Hubert Humphrey 33.8% G. Mennen Williams 11.3% John F. Kennedy 8.5% Stuart Symington 7.1% Pat Brown 4.2% Chester Bowles 1.4% With a much smaller return, the Negro Democrats overwhelmingly endorsed ex-Gov. Adlai Stevenson of Illinois and Sen. Hubert Humphrey of Minnesota, while giving the Party's hardest runner, Massachusetts Sen. John F. Kennedy, a poor fourth. In questioning the Democrats concerning their preference for opposition candidates on the Republican ticket, it is interesting to note that the Negro Democrats reciprocated with the Republicans by endorsing the candidates preferred by the Negro Republicans. The results were: Richard Nixon 63.1% Nelson Rockefeller 36.9% The poll brings out that many of the Democratic candidates do not enjoy a wide contact with Negroes across the country. The surprising strength for Stevenson and Humphrey probably is due in part to their outspokenness on civil rights and in part to their long relationship with Negro groups. Of Stevenson, the praise was great. Said a Montgomery, Ala., leader: "He is the only great statesman in the political arena. He is beyond a mere politician. He has great vision." Of Humphrey, a Norfolk, Va., pollster said: "A 17 Copyrighted material

Say Civil Rights Will Be Top Issue In '60 Race liberal who thinks." The popularity of the Vice President among the Negro Democrats could pose a real threat to the Party in the event it should nominate a candidate who is not as well known. The results possibly could be a loss of the traditional 80 per cent of the Negro vote. In response to the question: "What issue do you believe will be foremost to Negroes in the 1960 race?", the most typical reply was the tremendous emphasis on civil rights, while, on the other hand, only a few included economics or job opportunity or foreign affairs. Some of the issues mentioned follow. Said one Lansing, Mich., leader: "Executive leadership or the lack of it in bringing an orderly end to segregation, North and South." Said a Minneapolis leader: "Civil rights and the support of the Supreme Court order on segregation." Said a Los Angeles spokesman: "Civil rights and labor." Said a Philadelphia man: "School and housing desegregation." Leaders of both parties concurred wholeheartedly on the general subject of civil rights although some specified items such as Dixie voting barriers, while others mentioned the enforcement of the Supreme Court desegregation order for schools. This emphasis on civil rights is further enhanced by the similarity in the Presidential choices for both parties. Except for Sen. John Kennedy being on the GOP Big Three, both the Negro Dems and GOPers endorsed each other's candidates as their preferred opposition — indicating civil rights as a common denominator among Negro political leaders in the 1960 race. EDUCATION Little Rock High Schools To Integrate 6 Four Little Rock high schools, closed for a year by Gov. Orval Faubus to block integration, were slated to open with at least two of them racially integrated. The School Board approved attendance of three Negroes at Central High, scene of mob violence in 1957, and three more at Hall High in the city's elite district. Meanwhile, the last of Little Rock's three private, all-white high schools closed because of financial difficulties. WHITE MOTHER ASKS TOLERANCE Almost no one knew white, 12-year-old William Merrill Bodenheimer III until Houston (Texas) police pried his nude body from an ice box in an abandoned shack last month. And because he had been sexually abused before suffocating, police were quick to caution parents against hysteria and launched one of the city's greatest manhunts for a sex fiend. Prime suspect: a 25-year-old white man who twice tried to lure small girls into his automobile in the area where the Bodenheimer boy's body was found. Then police suddenly reversed their field, came up with the startling charge that the horrible crime had been committed by seven Negro boys ranging in age from 13 to 17 years and offered their signed confessions as evidence. What had been hysteria was replaced by cold fury, and whites talked of nothing else, tension grew. And while five of the youths were in custody of juvenile authorities, the court quickly set September 21 as the murder trial date for Adrian Johnson and Joe Edward Smith, both 17. Still tempers soared. In the first two weeks after her son's murder, Doris Bodenheimer, a 33-year-old nurse shared her counsel with no one, then, after watching the angry turn of events, sadly wrote an open letter to a Houston paper. It said: "Since my son's death I have been largely sustained by the sincere sympathy of the whole community, but the attitude on the part of some of them has alarmed me. I have felt from the beginning that some abstract evil force had killed my son. The person or persons reMrs. Bodenheimer had sorrow, no bitterness over son William's death. Copyrighted material

Dr. Thompson declines judgment, only wants best defense for boy. Youths Say Police Denied Them Lie Detector Tests guilt of the seven accused, so were many others in doubt. THE WAY I SEE IT By ROY CAMPANELLA With the hue and cry that spreads over sports pages these days about a proposed third major league, one thing about it is as sure as Hank Aaron's batting eye. There'll be more room for Negro ball players in bigmoney baseball. It happened in the late 40s, when the American and National Leagues opened their gates, and it'll be even more true if the Continental League gets into play in 1961. Watching baseball in the Negro leagues over the years, I've seen a good many players who really deserved major league contracts. But because of assorted reasons they could never find employment in the big-time. I can think of 10 or a dozen offhand who own all of the qualifications. Some have an age factor against them — too many years in baseball. A few others are young and unseasoned. Not ready yet, even for the minors. So the Negro leagues are still an untapped source for some of the 200-odd professionals the Continentals will need to begin full-scale operation. It is already certain that American and National League owners will be more than reluctant to waive rights on the players they now control. Maybe it'll take an act of Congress to loosen them up. One angle, for example, is to set a limit on the number of players a club can legally own. Some of the clubs today, with highly-developed farm systems, own rights on 300 to 400 players. If Congress rewrites the law so that each club can control only 75 to 80 players it's a sure bet that some of these will be free for play in the league. 54 Co aterial

BUSINESS Owner Says Sole Negro Linotype School Bankrupt The nation's only Negro owned school for typesetters, the Empire Linotype School, Inc., filed a petition for bankruptcy in New York Federal Court, listing debts of $109,370 and assets of $129,617. The firm's president and director, Edwin G. Jenkins, said he bought the school for $50,000 in 1950, but has been unable to afford enough advertising to attract new students. The school is equipped to handle 450 students, but now has only 15 trainees, providing only $3,200 a month in income. Alabama-born Jenkins, a one-time printer and typesetter, is in debt to two Wall St. mortgage firms for nearly $80,000. Also among his 10 top creditors are Harlem beauty shop owner Rose Morgan (for $2,300) and Negro lawyer William Raines ($2,500). NAACP Stops Picketing Of N. Y. Liquor Stores The New York NAACP withdrew picket lines from in front of Harlem liquor stores, but announced it was stepping up its drive to end discrimination against Negro wholesale liquor salesmen by seeking negotiations for an equitable system which totally disregards race in the assignment of sales personnel. The branch previously announced that there are only 72 Negro wholesale liquor salesmen out of a total of 1,800 in the city. 58 Copyrighted

ENTERTAINMENT London Audiences Praise 'Raisin9; Critics Cool The London premiere of A Raisin In The Sun, Lorraine Hansberry's prize-winning drama, was acclaimed by the audience but received little praise from the press critics. While most liked the acting — particularly Juanita Moore as the mother and Earle Hyman as the son — the reviewers thought the pace too slow. Alan Dent said in The News Chronicle that Miss Hansberry "will obviously write a better play when she learns to go straight forward and not round and round." The Daily Telegraph's W. A. Darlington called its development "slow and sometimes dull." ■ Ill MOVIE OF THE WEEK Teen-age lovers in Blue Denim. BLUE DENIM Arthur Bartley (Brandon DeWilde), wearing blue denim jeans that are the uniform of American teen-agers, pokes disconsolately into the empty dog house in his parents' backyard. He is suffering from his first jolt of sorrow. Without consulting him, his father, Maj. Malcolm Bartley, retired (MacDonald Carey), had a vet dispose of his aged dog. The incident which opens Blue Denim, a 20th CenturyFox film, points up the lack of rapport between father and son. This serious defect, a common one among parents of teen-age children, results in a series of misadventures and a tragedy. In his search for understanding and affection, Arthur turns to a schoolmate, Janet (Carol Lynley), who can't confide in her widower father. The two youngsters fall in love, experiment with sex, and Janet becomes pregnant. Appalled and frightened, Arthur and Janet try to get married in a neighboring town, but this fails when the marriage license clerk suspects they are under 21 and demands a birth certificate. An abortion seems to be the only solution for their problem, and in desperation Arthur, Janet and a pal, Ernie (Warren Berlinger), try to raise the needed $150. In the end Arthur forges his father's name to a check and confesses when discovered. Janet eventually is saved from undergoing the illegal operation. 65 Copyrighted material

Mississippi Faces Loss Of Seat In Congress Thirteen states, including Mississippi, face the loss of House seats and presidential electoral votes because of population shifts and the granting of Statehood to Alaska and Hawaii. Mississippi will lose one, New York and Pennsylvania, hardest hit, will lose three seats each.

WILL MARRIAGE HURT DANDRIDGE'S CAREER? CAREER ALWAYS SECONDARY After more than four years of saying "no," last fortnight singing screen star Dorothy Dandridge said **yes," to the umpteenth marriage proposal of her favorite guy, Beverly Hills restaurateur and night club owner Jack Denison. "No one was more surprised than I," said the white-haired, 43year-old Greek-CanaDorothy appears apprehensive in dian. "It was just beflrst and only photos of pair together, fore she left for England. We were having dinner and I said: *God, honey, how I want to marry you. Can't we get married as soon as you get back?' She said 'yes.' So we'll be married in a Greek Orthodox Church on June 21 and probably honeymoon for a couple of weeks in either Mexico or San Francisco, which ever she wants." And though Denison said Dorothy's "yes" made him "completely happy" for the first time in his life, the gossipers and prophets of doom were asking the question last week from Los Angeles to Long Island: Will marriage to Jack Denison hurt Dandridge's career? But, as if she had anticipated the question and mulled it around for quite some time, Dorothy told JET the night before leaving for England: "I don't think there is any connection between a career and marriage. I mean there are many career women who are happily married. If I were married today and had a contract to do this picture I would keep it. No, now don't misunderstand, I know 14

TO HUSBAND, SAYS SCREEN STAR that sounds cold and heartless, but what I'm trying to say is that if a lady doctor had to perform an operation in the morning, she kisses her husband goodbye and goes and performs the operation." Possessed of a keen mind, but extremely sensitive about her personal life, Dorothy concluded: "Anyway I don't believe a woman's career is ever primary if she loves her husband." But if Dorothy was somewhat inarticulate, husband-tobe Denison was not. Said he: "How is marriage going to hurt her career? I am not going to insist that she give it up because I don't want her to say in a couple of years I made her give it up. She'll probably make a few more pictures, do TV things or what ever she wants. "And if they're talking about our marriage being an interracial one, let me say we'll never honeymoon in Little Rock and probably never visit Atlanta. But look at "God honey how I want to marry you" Denison said. Then she answered "yes. Vll marry you as soon as I return from England." IS c

In fact, for othy sailed to English film date, ^^isit matter, who would have ever known that such a person as Jack Denison even existed if it hadn't been for Dorothy Dandridge? I don't think there's such a thing as a pure Caucasian. Take me, I don't know what I am. How do I know my Greek ancestors didn't consort with Africans or how do I know who they were? At least Dorothy knows what she is. "They ask if marrying me will hurt Dorothy's career and I tell them no, but if I'm wrong, it still will not make any diflerence, because I bought this club so that Dorothy will always be able to live in the manner she is accustomed to and we will never be beholden to anybody. EDUCATION Negro Girl Named Valedictorian At Okla. School A Negro student of Hartshorne (Okla.) High School, Sallye Phillips, who transferred to the formerly all-white school when it was integrated three years ago, was named class valedictorian. Senior Named ^Woman Of The Year* A senior at Northeastern University in Boston, Mass., was named "Woman of the Year," and became the first Negro to receive the award. MEDICINE N. Y. Senator Finds Medic Who Saved His Leg In War Fifteen years after an Army surgeon saved the leg of a badly wounded Negro soldier who had been hit by German shell fire in Northern Italy, New York State Sen. James Watson sought out Dr. Albert Flankers of Dubuque, Iowa, and expressed his appreciation. Dr. Flankers, Sen. Watson Ever since his return in 1944, Senator Watson said he had searched for the doctor, thinking he was a Dr. Plank of Albany. Publicity about his search resulted in a telephone call to Dr. Plankers, and the doctor remembered the battlefield operation. Ike's Physician To Study African Heart Disease Arriving in Johannesburg, South Africa, Dr. Paul Dudley White, physician to President Eisenhower, visited the Barawanath Hospital to study a heart disease which is rare among Europeans and common among Africans. Dr. White, who once operated upon President Eisenhower for a heart condition, said the African disease causes primary failure of a powerful heart muscle without damaging the valves or coronary arteries. BOOKS Brown's ^Trumbull Park' Enters 2nd Printing The first novel of Ebony associate editor Frank London Brown, entitled Trumbull Park, has sold 9,000 copies in advance of publication (April 13) , and will enter a second printing on April 3, the Henry Regnery Publishers disclosed in Chicago. It was the largest advance sale in the history of the company. Meanwhile, negotiations are underway with movie producer-director Otto Preminger for movie rights, for which the 31-year-old Brown will write the script. 19

FOREIGN NEWS 200 Die In Haiti Famine; 45,000 Threatened An estimated 200 persons died of starvation and 45,000 lives were reported threatened in a famine which hit Haiti's droughtstricken north-west coastal region. At Jean Rabel, center of the disaster, poor or non - existing roads hampered the distribution of 6,000 tons of U. S. Government foodstuff by Catholic and Protestant welfare organizations. People in affected areas reportedly have lost all hope because, even in the event of rain, they no longer have seed left for planting or animals to sell for seed. Haitian receives food. They are Dr. Gaston Faumelle, Jean V. Cauvin, Joseph L. Dejean, Antonio R. G. Petit, Seymour Pilet and Clevaux Auguste. Alan Lennox-Boyd in London. The appointment followed sustained criticism by London officials of Gov. Sir Robert Armitage^s failure to prove his allegations of a planned massacre of whites by the African National Congress. 21

ff HY CUBAN REBELS JAILED SOi\ OF WEALTHY NEGRO YOUNG TRAVELER WAS MISTAKEN By MARC CRAWFORD Early on the morning of February 16, eight plainclothesmen led by rebel Army Capt. Enrique Jimenez carried out a raid at a second-story apartment on Gervacio St. They confiscated one machine gun and three hand grenades; arrested 34-year-old Dominican Republic exile named Tomas Reyes Cerda, and a tall (fi-4) , handsome American Negro, who looks like a Cuban and speaks fluent Spanish like a Mexican. He was George McKissick Jones, a resident of Mexico City for 13 years and the elder son of Edward Jones, one of the two remaining Jones brothers, who were the Chicago South Side policy kings of the 30s and 40s. (The underworld cartel reputedly handled $10 million annually.) Taken to Camp Libertad Army Post, Jones and Cerda were jailed, held 38 days, then released from "investigation" without being charged. They had been suspected of being espionage agents from dictator Trujillo's DominiIdealistic young George Jones (I.) feels compulsion to oppose dictators. Edward Jones (rj said his son committed no crime. 22

AS AN AGENT FOR TRUJILLO With friend and prison-mate Cerda, Jones studies map of Caribbean area, with emphasis on Dominican Republic, Cerda is leader. can Republic. Fact was the pair was anti-Trujillo, had arrived in Havana January 28 from Mexico City, planned to promote a revolution of their own against the oldest dictator in Latin America. Cerda claimed they were accused by a rival Dominican anti-Trujilloist group, whose leader "wants to be the Fidel Castro of the Dominican Republic.'* The arms confiscated when they were arrested were part of a cache fellow revolutionaries were gathering. Former Mexico City College student (economics) Jones, a world traveler (last year he went to Spain, Italy and France "just to talk to people") who sometimes works as a salesman for his father's clothing factory at $100 a month, met Cerda in Mexico several years ago, was impressed with his dedication, offered to fight in his cause. "I don't like dictators wherever they are," Jones said, speaking with a slight accent and Latin passion, "whether in Dominican Republic, Haiti or Little Rock." 23 Ci

Jones Says He Dislikes Little Rock Dictators, Too The curly-headed 225pounder, who wanted to be a Catholic priest a few years ago and whose good looks now make Cuban girls turn around in the street, said: "I still admire Castro. He hates racial discrimination. I have no complaints about my arrest. In a revolution you can't take chances. Half of the battle of life, if not all, is to have a worthwhile cause. In 1935 Trujillo slew 10,000 Haitians. My family might not like what I'm doing, and I don't want to cause them any scandal. My father hates publicity, but I must do what I feel is right. I will not change." In Havana cafe, Jones (I.) discusses future with fellow revolu^ tionaireSy Dominican and Cuban. Cerda is third from right. Cl:

HE WAY I SEE IT . By Roy Campanella Maybe I'm jumping a gun by a few months and putting myself on a limb likely to be hacked off behind me, but I've got an early spring hunch which convinces me that 20year-old Vada Pinson of the Cincinnati Reds may cop "Rookie of the Year" honors this season. A slightly-built, lightning-fast center fielder, young Pinson came up from Seattle, the Red's Pacific Coast League farm team, where in '58 he hit .343 — including eleven home runs and 77 RBI's. At spring training in Tampa, Pinson has been the fireball of the 'Citrus Circuit," banging out base hits and snatching potential safe hits away from the best of the sluggers in the National League. To tell the truth, Pinson is not really new in the major leagues. Last season he opened the campaign with the Reds, played 27 games and hit a respectable .271. But one day in Pittsburgh he poled out a grandslam homer and then kind of fell apart. The pressure of playing in the big-time was too much for the raw kid. Wisely, Manager Mayo Smith sent him back to Seattle for more schooling. Pinson stands just under six feet and weighs a skimpy 170 pounds, but he's got the power of a much bigger man. In Cincinnati's exhibition opener against the Chicago White Sox he hit another grand-slam homer in the bottom of the ninth, then got so excited watching the ball that he passed a runner on first base and was credited only with a single, but his fence-busting single won the game for the Reds, 4-3. 54

LESTER YOVm: TRAGEDY OF A JAZZ GEMVS TREZ' SWAM AGAINST CURRENT, By ALLAN MORRISON Room 506-B at the Alvin Hotel, 52nd and Broadway, is large and dingy, with a cramped kitchenette, a worn sofa, shabby yellow drapes and a sprawling double bed. In that room Lester Willis Young, 49, familiarly called "Prez" throughout the jazz world and acknowledged as the most powerful influence on tenor saxophone playing in the past 20 years, passed his last hours. On that bed around 3:30 a.m., March 15, he died of a heart seizure, undoubtedly triggered by acute cirrhosis. Burial took Lester Young's drinking habit hastened his death Funeral service attracted jazz greats and family, including widow, Mary (l.J, son Lester Jr., drummer Jo Jones (rj. 58 Copyrighted material

INFLUENCED 2 JAZZ GENERATIONS place March 19, in Evergreen Cemetery. Mourned in many lands, the lovable man with the sensitive soul, who wore flamboyant pork pie hats and a Bohemian mane of hair, joined the immortals of jazz. All his life Young swam against the current, rejected convention, thumbed his nose at society and inhabited a world of his own making — remote, strange, inaccessible. With little formal training but endowed with immense talent and a rare flair for doing the unusual, he played perhaps the decisive single role in changing the course of American jazz, and in so doing left an imperishable imprint upon his country's culture. His place in jazz history can be gauged by the results of a "musicians* musicians" poll conducted by jazz critic Leonard Feather for his 1956 Encyclopedia Yearbook Of Jazz. Of 101 leading U. S, jazz musicians polled 52 named him "greatest ever" in the tenor saxophone category. Born into a musical family in Woodville, Miss., August, 1909, Lester arrived at his musical eminence in stages. Passing Lester's bier, blind singer Al Hibbler (L) sang "In The Garden," trombonist Tyree Gleen played instrumental solo. 59 Ci iterial

Lester's old friend, Billie Holiday, wept at services; tenor saxophonist Illinois Jacquet was another grief -stricken mourner, Lester Was Drummer At 10, Left Dad's Carnival At 18 From 10 to 13 he was a drummer. From 14 to 18 he played the alto saxophone, and when he was 18 he ran away from his father's carnival group and joined Art Bronson's Bostonians as a baritone sax player. He shifted to the tenor while with this band and stayed with that instrument until death. His last tenor saxophone, a gleaming gold-plated horn, was unceremoniously carted off to the nearest police precinct, later to be claimed by his widow. He was the first important performer to challenge the supremacy of the Hawkins style, and later Billie Holiday dubbed him ^'President of the Tenor Saxophone." He became "President of the Republic of the Tenor Saxophone" by virtue of his leadership of the musical revolution that swept through the 30*s and culminated in what is now the modern jazz movement. His ex-boss Count Basie, said: "Prez was one of the finest musicians IVe known, and it will be a long time belore there will be another one to fill his shoes in his 60

style of playing— the style that made him inimitable." Lester was uprooted by World War n and maimed spiritually and physically by searing brutality experienced in an Army guardhouse in Alabama. The Lester Young who returned from the war was an altered being— embittered, withdrawn, cynical. Always a gay, high liver, the post-war Lester was moodier, more reliant on such escape valves as liquor and other indulgences sometimes associated with musicians. Once it got under way his descent gathered merciless momentum. Along the way he reaped certain commercial rewards. Between 1946 and the end he never earned less than $25,000 a year. Peak year in terms of earnings was 1946-47 when his income topped the $75,000 level. For his last tour, a four-week "gig" in the Blue Note Club in Paris he was paid $500 a week. From 1946 to 1955, the year he was tricked into presenting himself for treatment at New York's Bellevue Hospital, Lester's drinking progressively increased in volume to the point where he was consuming 12 fifths a week. At Bellevue his condition was diagnosed as early cirrhosis of the liver and incipient alcoholism. An examining psychiatrist, who happened to be one of his fans, recorded that his liquor had seriously slowed his mental processes. After his discharge from the hospital he made a valiant attempt to stop drinking "until I'm well and strong enough to start drinking again/' Six months later he climbed off the wagon, announcing: "I like to drink and I'm going to keep on drinking even if it kills me." The doctors told him that unless he stopped drinking he would die. "OK/' he told his closest friends, "I'll quit drinking but I intend to have a few more nips before I stop." For years Prez was a gin man, then he switched to Scotch. In the early 1950's he always kept two fifths in his instrument case. Lester's psychological problems were formidable, but he sternly resisted psychotherapy. ''The sooner I drink myself to death the happier I'll be," he shouted on more than one occasion. No one really knew the true Lester Young. The roots oi: 61 Copyrighted material

Lester Once Made Valiant Attempt To Stop Drinking Habit his unhappiness lay in deprived childhood, in two tragically unsuccessful marriages and in the traumatic terror of what happened to him in the Army. He tried to close out the reality of life by withdrawal into the awful world of the bottle. To those few friends able to pierce the barriers of his defenses Lester Young was an enigma, a man at war both with himself and society. The last 10 years of his life were characterized by desperate dissipation which took the form of sustained around-the-clock drinking which was interpreted by one psychiatrist as a pattern of self-destruction. Detached and uninhibited, he withdrew gradually into a tiny shell. He regarded humanity as one vast, cruel conspiracy against himself. Basically, he loved people. "Prez was the sweetest, kindest man who ever lived," says his ex-manager, Charlie Carpenter. "Lester had a deep death wish," observed one jazz critic. Another musician who knew him intimately commented: "He wanted to die in the worst way." Mrs. Basie (I), Jones and Earl Warren discuss funeral. ■ ■■■ MOVIE OF THE WEEK ■■■■ THE NAKED MAJA One of the most colorful eras in European history is brought back to life in The Naked Maja, a United Artists release starring Ava Gardner and Anthony Franciosa. The movie is based on the 18th Century, and two of its most dynamic personalities— Francisco Goya (Franciosa) , one of the world's greatest painters, and Maria, the thirteenth Duchess of Alba (Gardner), dubbed **the most notorious woman in Spanish history." The film, shot in Italy in colorful Technirama, is a daring and realistic re-creation of the tumultuous love affair of Goya and Maria that shook the Spain of their day to its foundations. The film's title refers to one of two famous pictures Goya painted of his mistress, the Duchess of Alba. One, ''the Maja Nude," shows Maria completely unclothed; the second, "Maja Robed," is the same in every detail except her clothing. The face in both film portraits is that of Ava Gardner. Also in The Naked Maja many of the celebrated and priceless works of Goya are seen on the screen for the first time. The youngest member of the cast is a six-year-old brown doll with lustrous amber eyes and a crown of black curls. She is Yemiko Fullwood, who plays the role of the precocious Negro orphan, Maria della Luz, whom the childless Duchess adopted. Yemiko is the daughter of GI Artis Fullwood, a native of Georgia, and a Japanese mother. 65
